stages.storyIntro: You stand at the entrance to the Pod Barracks — row upon row of dormant containers waiting to be scheduled. A spectral figure hovers above an empty node: The Pending Pod, endlessly waiting for resources that never come. KubeWise appears beside you: 'Before you can defeat it, you must understand what a Pod actually is — and why it gets stuck.'
stages.bossObjective: Defeat The Pending Pod by demonstrating mastery of Pod anatomy, resource requests, and the scheduling process.

concept.title: What is a Pod?
concept.summary: A Pod is the smallest deployable unit in Kubernetes. It wraps one or more containers that share the same network namespace and storage volumes. The Pod is the atom — everything else in Kubernetes schedules, manages, and scales Pods.

chunks.title: Pod Anatomy
chunks.content: A Pod contains: one or more containers (usually one), a shared network namespace (all containers in a Pod share the same IP and port space), optional shared volumes, and metadata (labels, annotations, name, namespace). Pods are ephemeral — they are not rescheduled if they die; a higher-level controller like a Deployment handles that.
keyPoints: One Pod = one IP address (shared by all containers in the Pod)
Containers in a Pod communicate via localhost
Pods are ephemeral — they do not self-heal
Labels are key-value pairs used by Services and Deployments to select Pods

chunks.title: Writing Your First Pod YAML
chunks.content: Every Kubernetes resource is defined as YAML. Here is a minimal Pod definition:
chunks.code:
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: my-first-pod
  labels:
    app: hello-world
spec:
  containers:
    - name: hello-container
      image: nginx:alpine
      ports:
        - containerPort: 80
      resources:
        requests:
          memory: "64Mi"
          cpu: "250m"
        limits:
          memory: "128Mi"
          cpu: "500m"
keyPoints: `apiVersion: v1` — Pods are a core API resource
`metadata.labels` — used by Services to route traffic to this Pod
`resources.requests` — what the scheduler reserves on a node
`resources.limits` — the hard ceiling; exceeding memory triggers OOMKill

chunks.title: Essential kubectl Commands for Pods
chunks.content: The kubectl CLI is your primary tool for interacting with the Kubernetes API. These are the commands you'll use most often when working with Pods:
chunks.code:
# Create a Pod from YAML
kubectl apply -f pod.yaml

# List all Pods in the current namespace
kubectl get pods

# List Pods with more detail (node, IP, age)
kubectl get pods -o wide

# Get full details and Events for a Pod (essential for debugging)
kubectl describe pod my-first-pod

# Stream live logs from a Pod
kubectl logs -f my-first-pod

# Open an interactive shell inside a running Pod
kubectl exec -it my-first-pod -- /bin/sh

# Delete a Pod
kubectl delete pod my-first-pod
keyPoints: `kubectl describe` — always check the Events section at the bottom when debugging
`kubectl logs -f` — the `-f` flag streams logs in real time
`kubectl exec -it` — your escape hatch into a running container
A Pod in `Pending` state has not been scheduled — check resource availability

quest.title: Schedule the First Pod
quest.objective: Create a Pod named `nginx-scout` using the `nginx:alpine` image with resource requests of 64Mi memory and 250m CPU.
quest.description: The Barracks needs its first occupant. Write a Pod manifest and apply it to the cluster. Confirm it reaches the Running state.
acceptanceCriteria: Resource requests include memory: 64Mi and cpu: 250m
hints: Use `kubectl get pods` to check the status
If the Pod is Pending, run `kubectl describe pod nginx-scout` and read the Events

variants.question: What is the smallest deployable unit in Kubernetes?

options.text: Container
Pod
Deployment
Node

variants.explanation: A Pod is the smallest deployable unit. Containers run inside Pods. Pods are scheduled onto Nodes. Deployments manage Pods.

variants.question: Two containers are in the same Pod. How do they communicate with each other?

options.text: Via a Kubernetes Service
Via localhost, since they share a network namespace
Via the Pod's ClusterIP
They cannot — containers in a Pod are isolated from each other

variants.explanation: Containers in the same Pod share a network namespace, meaning they share the same IP address and can reach each other on localhost with different ports.

variants.question: A Pod is stuck in `Pending` state. `kubectl describe pod my-pod` shows the event: 'Insufficient memory'. What is the most likely cause?

options.text: The container image does not exist in the registry
The Pod's resource requests exceed the available allocatable memory on all nodes
The Pod's namespace does not exist
The kubelet on the target node is not running

variants.explanation: The Kubernetes scheduler checks resource requests (not limits) when deciding where to place a Pod. If no node has enough allocatable memory to satisfy the request, the Pod remains Pending. Check node capacity with `kubectl describe nodes`.

stages.storyIntro: The Pod Barracks is secured. But the pods are islands — powerful, but silent. CTRL flickers into view: 'Running pods are invisible to the outside world without a Service. And the CrashLoop Specter feeds on Services with broken selectors — endpoints empty, traffic going nowhere. Let's fix that.'
stages.bossObjective: Defeat The CrashLoop Specter by creating both a ClusterIP and a NodePort service with correctly matched label selectors.

concept.title: Kubernetes Services
concept.summary: A Service is a stable networking abstraction over a dynamic set of Pods. Because Pods are ephemeral (their IPs change when they restart), Services provide a consistent IP and DNS name. Services use label selectors to find their target Pods — if the selector doesn't match any Pod labels, the Service has no endpoints.

chunks.title: Service Types
chunks.content: Kubernetes has four Service types. The two you'll use most as a beginner are ClusterIP and NodePort. ClusterIP is internal-only (within the cluster). NodePort exposes the service on every node's IP at a static port.
keyPoints: ClusterIP: default type, accessible only within the cluster. A stable internal IP and DNS name.
NodePort: opens a port (30000-32767) on every node, making the service accessible from outside the cluster.
LoadBalancer: provisions a cloud load balancer (AWS ELB, GCP LB, etc.) — cloud-specific.
ExternalName: maps a service to a DNS name — rarely used at the beginner level.

chunks.title: Service YAML and the Selector
chunks.content: The most critical part of a Service is the selector. It must match the labels on the target Pods exactly. Here are examples of ClusterIP and NodePort services:
chunks.code:
# ClusterIP Service (internal only)
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: hello-service
spec:
  type: ClusterIP
  selector:
    app: hello-world   # Must match Pod's metadata.labels.app
  ports:
    - protocol: TCP
      port: 80         # Port the Service listens on
      targetPort: 80   # Port the Pod container is listening on

---
# NodePort Service (external access)
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: hello-nodeport
spec:
  type: NodePort
  selector:
    app: hello-world
  ports:
    - protocol: TCP
      port: 80
      targetPort: 80
      nodePort: 30080  # Optional: specify port, or let K8s assign one
keyPoints: The `selector` field must exactly match at least one running Pod's labels
`port` is what clients connect to on the Service
`targetPort` is the port the container is actually listening on — these can differ
Check endpoints with `kubectl get endpoints <service-name>` — empty endpoints mean selector mismatch

chunks.title: Creating Services with kubectl
chunks.content: You can create a Service imperatively (without writing YAML) using `kubectl expose`. This is useful for quick testing, though YAML is preferred for production.
chunks.code:
# Expose a Pod as a ClusterIP service
kubectl expose pod nginx-scout --port=80 --target-port=80 --name=nginx-service

# Expose a Deployment as a NodePort service
kubectl expose deployment my-app --type=NodePort --port=80

# List all services
kubectl get services
# Or shorthand:
kubectl get svc

# Check if a Service has any endpoints (critical for debugging)
kubectl get endpoints nginx-service

# Describe the Service — shows selector, endpoints, and events
kubectl describe svc nginx-service
keyPoints: `kubectl expose` creates a Service based on the resource's labels
`kubectl get endpoints` — if this shows `<none>`, your selector is wrong
DNS within the cluster: `<service-name>.<namespace>.svc.cluster.local`
Within the same namespace, you can just use `<service-name>` as the hostname

quest.title: Open the Gateway
quest.objective: Create a ClusterIP Service named `gateway-service` that selects Pods with the label `app: nginx-scout` on port 80.
quest.description: The Pod Barracks has been secured, but the Pods are still unreachable. Create a Service to expose them to the rest of the cluster.
acceptanceCriteria: `kubectl get endpoints gateway-service` shows at least one endpoint
hints: Make sure your Pod from Stage 1 has the label `app: nginx-scout`
Use `kubectl get endpoints gateway-service` to verify the selector is working

variants.question: Which Service type makes an application accessible from outside the Kubernetes cluster on a fixed port on every node?

options.text: ClusterIP
NodePort
ExternalName
InternalIP

variants.explanation: NodePort opens a port in the range 30000-32767 on every node's IP, making the Service reachable from outside the cluster.

variants.question: You create a Service with `selector: app: my-app`, but `kubectl get endpoints my-service` shows no endpoints. What is the most likely cause?

options.text: The Service has not been applied to the cluster yet
No running Pods have the label `app: my-app`
The `port` field in the Service spec is incorrect
The Kubernetes DNS plugin is not running

variants.explanation: A Service populates its Endpoints list by finding Pods that match its selector AND are in the Ready state. If no Pods match the selector, the endpoints list is empty.

variants.question: A Service has `port: 80` and `targetPort: 8080`. A Pod in the cluster makes an HTTP request to `my-service:80`. What port does the traffic arrive on inside the target Pod's container?

options.text: The nodePort value
The request fails because the ports don't match

variants.explanation: The Service listens on `port` (80) and forwards traffic to `targetPort` (8080) on the Pod. This allows the Service's public port to differ from the container's actual listening port — useful when you can't control what port an application uses.

stages.storyIntro: The Service Gateway is open. But every Pod you've created so far is a single, fragile soldier — if it crashes, it's gone. KubeWise steps forward: 'This is why the Orchestration Order invented the Deployment. It does not manage individual Pods — it manages the desired state. Tell it you want three replicas, and three you shall have. Always.' The Deployment Daemon laughs from the shadows, spawning endless ReplicaSets.
stages.bossObjective: Defeat The Deployment Daemon by creating a Deployment, scaling it, performing a rolling update, and executing a rollback.

concept.title: Deployments
concept.summary: A Deployment is a higher-level controller that manages a set of identical Pods via a ReplicaSet. You declare your desired state (e.g. 3 replicas of nginx:1.25) and the Deployment controller works to make reality match that state — creating, deleting, or updating Pods as needed.

chunks.title: Creating a Deployment
chunks.content: A Deployment YAML includes a Pod template inside it. The Deployment uses this template to create and manage Pods. The `replicas` field controls how many identical Pods should be running at all times.
chunks.code:
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: web-deployment
  labels:
    app: web
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: web
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: web  # Must match spec.selector.matchLabels
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: web
          image: nginx:1.25
          ports:
            - containerPort: 80
          resources:
            requests:
              memory: "64Mi"
              cpu: "250m"
            limits:
              memory: "128Mi"
              cpu: "500m"
keyPoints: `spec.replicas` — the desired number of Pod replicas; the Deployment always tries to maintain this count
`spec.selector.matchLabels` — how the Deployment identifies its own Pods; must match `spec.template.metadata.labels`
Deleting a Pod that belongs to a Deployment causes the Deployment to create a replacement immediately
The Deployment creates a ReplicaSet internally — you rarely interact with ReplicaSets directly

chunks.title: Scaling and Rolling Updates
chunks.content: Scaling a Deployment and updating its image are both declarative operations. You can do both imperatively with kubectl or by editing the YAML and re-applying.
chunks.code:
# Create a deployment imperatively
kubectl create deployment web-deployment --image=nginx:1.25 --replicas=3

# Scale a Deployment
kubectl scale deployment web-deployment --replicas=5

# Update the container image (triggers a rolling update)
kubectl set image deployment/web-deployment web=nginx:1.26

# Watch the rollout status in real time
kubectl rollout status deployment/web-deployment

# View rollout history
kubectl rollout history deployment/web-deployment

# Rollback to the previous version
kubectl rollout undo deployment/web-deployment

# Rollback to a specific revision
kubectl rollout undo deployment/web-deployment --to-revision=1
keyPoints: Rolling update: Kubernetes replaces Pods one at a time, ensuring availability during the update
`kubectl rollout status` — blocks until the rollout completes or fails; great for CI/CD pipelines
`kubectl rollout undo` — instant rollback to the previous known-good state
Revision history is limited by `spec.revisionHistoryLimit` (default: 10)

chunks.title: Update Strategies
chunks.content: The Deployment's update strategy controls how Pods are replaced during a rollout. The default strategy is RollingUpdate.
keyPoints: RollingUpdate (default): gradually replaces old Pods with new ones. Configurable via `maxSurge` and `maxUnavailable`.
Recreate: terminates ALL old Pods before creating new ones. Causes downtime but avoids running mixed versions.
`maxSurge`: how many extra Pods above the desired count can exist during a rollout (e.g. 1 or 25%)
`maxUnavailable`: how many Pods can be unavailable during a rollout (e.g. 0 for zero-downtime deploys)

quest.title: Storm the Core
quest.objective: Create a Deployment named `citadel-web` with 3 replicas of `nginx:1.25`, then perform a rolling update to `nginx:1.26`, and verify the rollout completes successfully.
quest.description: The Deployment Daemon spawns endless ReplicaSets to confuse you. Show it you understand how Deployments work by creating one, scaling it, and updating it cleanly.
acceptanceCriteria: Rollout history shows at least 2 revisions
hints: Use `kubectl rollout status` after the image update to watch progress
If something goes wrong, `kubectl rollout undo deployment/citadel-web` reverts it

variants.question: You delete one of the three Pods in a Deployment. What happens?

options.text: The Deployment scales down to 2 replicas permanently
The Deployment creates a new Pod to replace the deleted one
Nothing — Deployments don't monitor individual Pods
The entire Deployment is restarted

variants.explanation: The Deployment controller continuously compares the current state (2 Pods) with the desired state (3 replicas) and creates a new Pod to reconcile the difference.

variants.question: What kubectl command updates the container image in a Deployment named `my-app`, container named `app`, to `my-image:v2`?

options.text: `kubectl update deployment my-app --image=my-image:v2`
`kubectl set image deployment/my-app app=my-image:v2`
`kubectl rollout image deployment/my-app my-image:v2`
`kubectl patch deployment my-app image=my-image:v2`

variants.explanation: `kubectl set image deployment/<name> <container-name>=<image>:<tag>` is the imperative command for updating a container image in a Deployment. The container name must match the name defined in the Deployment's pod template.

variants.question: A Deployment has `maxUnavailable: 0` and `maxSurge: 1`. It currently runs 4 replicas. During a rolling update, what is the maximum number of Pods that will exist at any one time?
variants.explanation: `maxSurge: 1` means at most 1 extra Pod above the desired count (4) can exist — so a maximum of 5 Pods. `maxUnavailable: 0` means no existing Pod is removed until the new Pod is Ready, ensuring zero downtime.

stages.storyIntro: The Deployment Daemon is defeated. But the ConfigMap Vault looms ahead — its corrupted configs are poisoning applications across the cluster. CTRL's voice crackles: 'Hard-coding configuration into images is how the Corruptor wins. ConfigMaps separate config from code. Learn them, and no environment variable can be weaponized against you.'
stages.bossObjective: Defeat The ConfigMap Corruptor by correctly injecting a ConfigMap as both an environment variable and a mounted volume file.

concept.title: ConfigMaps
concept.summary: A ConfigMap stores non-sensitive configuration data as key-value pairs. It decouples configuration from container images, allowing the same image to run with different configs in dev, staging, and production. ConfigMap data can be injected into Pods as environment variables or as files in a volume mount.

chunks.title: Creating ConfigMaps
chunks.content: ConfigMaps can be created from literal values, files, or YAML manifests. Here are the most common methods:
chunks.code:
# Create from literal values
kubectl create configmap app-config \
  --from-literal=APP_ENV=production \
  --from-literal=LOG_LEVEL=info \
  --from-literal=MAX_CONNECTIONS=100

# Create from a file (key = filename, value = file contents)
kubectl create configmap nginx-conf --from-file=nginx.conf

# Create from a YAML manifest
# configmap.yaml:
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: app-config
data:
  APP_ENV: "production"
  LOG_LEVEL: "info"
  config.yaml: |
    server:
      port: 8080
      timeout: 30s

# Apply it
kubectl apply -f configmap.yaml

# View it
kubectl get configmap app-config -o yaml
keyPoints: ConfigMaps can store simple key=value pairs or entire file contents as values
Multi-line values use the YAML block scalar (`|`) syntax
ConfigMaps are namespace-scoped — they can only be accessed by Pods in the same namespace
For sensitive data (passwords, tokens), use Secrets instead of ConfigMaps

chunks.title: Injecting ConfigMaps as Environment Variables
chunks.content: You can inject ConfigMap keys as environment variables in a Pod's container spec. There are two approaches: inject specific keys or inject all keys at once.
chunks.code:
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: app-pod
spec:
  containers:
    - name: app
      image: my-app:latest
      env:
        # Inject a specific key from a ConfigMap
        - name: APP_ENV
          valueFrom:
            configMapKeyRef:
              name: app-config
              key: APP_ENV
      envFrom:
        # Inject ALL keys from a ConfigMap as env vars
        - configMapRef:
            name: app-config
keyPoints: `env[].valueFrom.configMapKeyRef` — injects a single key with a potentially different env var name
`envFrom[].configMapRef` — injects all keys from the ConfigMap; key names become env var names
Changes to a ConfigMap do NOT automatically update running Pods — you must restart them
If the referenced ConfigMap or key doesn't exist, the Pod will fail to start

chunks.title: Mounting ConfigMaps as Files
chunks.content: ConfigMaps can be mounted as a directory inside a container. Each key becomes a file in that directory, with the value as the file content. This is the preferred way to supply config files to applications.
chunks.code:
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: app-with-config-file
spec:
  volumes:
    - name: config-volume
      configMap:
        name: app-config   # Must match the ConfigMap name
  containers:
    - name: app
      image: my-app:latest
      volumeMounts:
        - name: config-volume
          mountPath: /etc/config   # Files appear here inside the container
keyPoints: Each key in the ConfigMap becomes a file at `mountPath/<key-name>`
When using a `subPath` mount, changes to the ConfigMap are NOT reflected live
Without `subPath`, ConfigMap volume mounts are updated live within ~1 minute
Mounting to an existing directory will hide other files in that directory — use a dedicated config dir

quest.title: Unlock the Vault
quest.objective: Create a ConfigMap named `vault-config` with keys `LOG_LEVEL=debug` and `MAX_RETRIES=5`. Then create a Pod that injects `LOG_LEVEL` as an environment variable AND mounts the entire ConfigMap as a volume at `/etc/vault-config`.
quest.description: The Vault's lock mechanism requires both injection methods. Prove you can use a ConfigMap both ways to unlock the vault and expose the Corruptor.
acceptanceCriteria: Pod has `LOG_LEVEL` available as an environment variable
Pod has the ConfigMap mounted at `/etc/vault-config`
Both `/etc/vault-config/LOG_LEVEL` and `/etc/vault-config/MAX_RETRIES` files exist inside the container
hints: Use `kubectl exec -it <pod> -- ls /etc/vault-config` to verify the mount
Use `kubectl exec -it <pod> -- env | grep LOG_LEVEL` to verify the env var

variants.question: You update a ConfigMap that is mounted as a volume in a running Pod. When will the Pod see the new values?

options.text: Immediately
Within approximately 1 minute (after the kubelet sync interval)
Never — you must restart the Pod
Only after the next Deployment rollout

variants.explanation: ConfigMap volumes are updated periodically by the kubelet (default sync interval ~1 minute). However, this does NOT apply to env vars injected from ConfigMaps — those only update on Pod restart.

variants.question: Which resource should you use instead of a ConfigMap to store a database password?

options.text: A ConfigMap with the `type: sensitive` field set
A Kubernetes Secret
An environment variable baked into the container image
A PersistentVolume with restricted access

variants.explanation: Kubernetes Secrets are designed for sensitive data. They are base64-encoded at rest (and can be encrypted at rest with proper cluster configuration), have RBAC controls, and are not exposed in `kubectl describe` output by default.

variants.question: A Pod references a ConfigMap key using `configMapKeyRef` with `optional: false` (the default). The ConfigMap exists but the specific key does not. What happens when you apply the Pod?

options.text: The Pod starts but the environment variable is empty
The Pod fails to start and reports a CreateContainerConfigError
Kubernetes automatically creates the missing key with an empty value
The Pod starts but is immediately restarted in a CrashLoopBackOff

variants.explanation: When `optional` is false (the default), a missing ConfigMap or a missing key in an existing ConfigMap will prevent the Pod from starting. The kubelet reports a `CreateContainerConfigError`. Set `optional: true` if you want the Pod to start even when the key is absent.

stages.storyIntro: The Vault is secure. You stand at the outer Battlements — the final sector. The Ingress Interceptor has seized control of all external routing. Traffic meant for the cluster's applications vanishes into a void. KubeWise appears one final time: 'Ingress is the gate. The Interceptor is its corruption. Master the rules of the gate, and the Citadel is yours.'
stages.bossObjective: Defeat The Ingress Interceptor by configuring an Ingress resource with at least two path rules and host-based routing that correctly reaches the backend Services.

concept.title: Ingress
concept.summary: An Ingress resource defines HTTP and HTTPS routing rules that direct external traffic to internal Services. Unlike a NodePort or LoadBalancer Service, a single Ingress can route traffic to multiple Services based on the hostname or URL path — making it the standard way to expose web applications in Kubernetes.

chunks.title: How Ingress Works
chunks.content: Ingress requires two components: an Ingress resource (the routing rules) and an Ingress Controller (the component that reads those rules and actually routes traffic). Common controllers include nginx-ingress, Traefik, and AWS ALB Ingress Controller.
keyPoints: Ingress ≠ Ingress Controller. The resource defines rules; the controller enforces them.
You must install an Ingress Controller before any Ingress resources take effect
Host-based routing: route `api.myapp.com` to one Service, `app.myapp.com` to another
Path-based routing: route `/api/` to one Service, `/` to another — on the same hostname
TLS termination: Ingress can terminate HTTPS using a Kubernetes Secret containing a TLS certificate

chunks.title: Writing an Ingress Resource
chunks.content: An Ingress resource defines `rules` — each rule specifies a host and a list of path-to-service mappings. The `ingressClassName` specifies which Ingress Controller should handle this resource.
chunks.code:
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Ingress
metadata:
  name: citadel-ingress
  annotations:
    nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/rewrite-target: /
spec:
  ingressClassName: nginx
  rules:
    # Host-based routing
    - host: app.citadel.local
      http:
        paths:
          - path: /
            pathType: Prefix
            backend:
              service:
                name: frontend-service
                port:
                  number: 80
          # Path-based routing on the same host
          - path: /api
            pathType: Prefix
            backend:
              service:
                name: api-service
                port:
                  number: 8080
  # TLS configuration
  tls:
    - hosts:
        - app.citadel.local
      secretName: citadel-tls-secret
keyPoints: `pathType: Prefix` — matches all requests starting with that path
`pathType: Exact` — matches only that exact path
More specific paths take precedence over less specific ones
`ingressClassName` must match the name of an installed Ingress Controller
Annotations customize controller-specific behavior (rewrites, rate limiting, auth, etc.)

chunks.title: Managing Ingress with kubectl
chunks.content: Commands for creating, inspecting, and debugging Ingress resources:
chunks.code:
# Apply an Ingress resource
kubectl apply -f ingress.yaml

# List all Ingress resources
kubectl get ingress
# Or:
kubectl get ing

# Describe an Ingress (shows rules and backend resolution)
kubectl describe ingress citadel-ingress

# Check Ingress Controller logs (for nginx-ingress)
kubectl logs -n ingress-nginx deployment/ingress-nginx-controller

# Test routing locally (if you've set /etc/hosts for the hostname)
curl -H 'Host: app.citadel.local' http://<node-ip>:<nodeport>/api
keyPoints: `kubectl describe ingress` shows which rules are active and the ADDRESS assigned by the controller
If ADDRESS is empty, the Ingress Controller is not running or not picking up the ingressClassName
Use `-H 'Host: ...'` with curl to test host-based routing without real DNS
Check Ingress Controller logs first when traffic isn't routing as expected

quest.title: Reclaim the Battlements
quest.objective: Create an Ingress named `battlements-ingress` on host `citadel.local` with two path rules: `/` routes to `frontend-service:80` and `/api` routes to `api-service:8080`.
quest.description: The Interceptor controls all routing. Define the Ingress rules that will take back control and correctly route traffic to the Citadel's internal services.
acceptanceCriteria: `kubectl describe ingress battlements-ingress` shows both rules
hints: Make sure the backend Services (`frontend-service` and `api-service`) exist before testing

variants.question: What component actually enforces Ingress routing rules in a Kubernetes cluster?

options.text: The kube-scheduler
The Ingress Controller
The Ingress resource itself
The kube-proxy

variants.explanation: An Ingress resource is just a configuration object. The Ingress Controller (e.g. nginx-ingress, Traefik) is the running component that reads Ingress resources and configures the actual load balancer/proxy to route traffic accordingly.

variants.question: An Ingress has two rules on the same host: path `/api` (Prefix) and path `/` (Prefix). A request comes in for `/api/users`. Which rule matches?

options.text: The `/` rule, because it comes first
The `/api` rule, because it is more specific
Both rules — the request is sent to both backends
Neither rule — there is a conflict and the request is rejected

variants.explanation: Ingress controllers match the most specific (longest) path first. `/api` is longer and more specific than `/`, so `/api/users` matches the `/api` rule and is routed to the `api-service` backend.

variants.question: You apply an Ingress resource and `kubectl get ingress` shows no ADDRESS after several minutes. What is the most likely explanation?

options.text: The backend Services referenced in the Ingress do not exist
No Ingress Controller is running in the cluster, or the `ingressClassName` does not match any controller
The Ingress resource has a syntax error
The cluster's cloud provider does not support Ingress

variants.explanation: The ADDRESS field is populated by the Ingress Controller after it processes the Ingress resource. If no controller is installed, or if the `ingressClassName` field doesn't match the controller's class name, the resource will sit unprocessed indefinitely with no ADDRESS assigned.

nodes.name: Pod Fundamentals
nodes.description: Understand Pod anatomy, lifecycle, and essential kubectl commands

nodes.name: Resource Management
nodes.description: Define CPU and memory requests/limits to ensure proper scheduling

nodes.name: Service Networking
nodes.description: Expose Pods via ClusterIP and NodePort services with correct selectors

nodes.name: Deployment Control
nodes.description: Create, scale, and update Deployments with rolling update strategies

nodes.name: Configuration Management
nodes.description: Inject ConfigMaps as env vars and volume mounts

nodes.description: Configure host-based and path-based routing for external traffic

nodes.name: Citadel Master
nodes.description: Complete mastery of the five Kubernetes fundamentals

content.code:
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: my-pod
  labels:
    app: web
spec:
  containers:
  - name: web
    image: nginx:alpine
    ports:
    - containerPort: 80
    resources:
      requests:
        memory: "64Mi"
        cpu: "100m"
      limits:
        memory: "128Mi"
        cpu: "500m"
content.caption: Every Pod needs resource requests and limits. Without them, the scheduler cannot place the Pod correctly.

content.text: Labels are key-value pairs attached to Kubernetes objects. Selectors find objects by their labels. A Service with selector `app: web` routes traffic to ALL pods with the label `app: web` — regardless of how many there are. This is the glue that holds Kubernetes together.

requests: what the scheduler uses to decide where to place the Pod. limits: the maximum the container can use. CPU throttles at the limit. Memory kills the container (OOMKill) if exceeded. Evicted Pod Wraiths are born when nodes run out of memory — always set both.

content.code:
strategy:
  type: RollingUpdate
  rollingUpdate:
    maxSurge: 1         # max extra pods above desired count
    maxUnavailable: 0   # min pods always available during update

# Rollback if something goes wrong:
kubectl rollout undo deployment/my-app

# Check history:
kubectl rollout history deployment/my-app
content.caption: maxUnavailable: 0 means zero downtime. Kubernetes adds new pods before removing old ones.

content.code:
# Inject as environment variables
env:
- name: DB_HOST
  valueFrom:
    configMapKeyRef:
      name: app-config
      key: database_host

# Or mount as a file
volumeMounts:
- name: config-volume
  mountPath: /etc/config
volumes:
- name: config-volume
  configMap:
    name: app-config
content.caption: Two ways to inject ConfigMaps: env vars (simple values) or volume mounts (files, configs with structure).
